Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mr. Raymond Pyke 
Hesperia Resorter 
P.O. Box 947 
Hesperia, CA 
92345 
Dear Mr. Pyke: 
October 19, 1976 
Thank you for your letter of October 12, 1976, request-
ing information concerning the manner in which free food 
and beverages contributed to a candidate should be reported 
on campaign statements required to be filed in accordance 
with the Political Reform Act. 
The law makes no distinction between state and local 
all "expenditures made at the behest of a 
f 
ttee or elected officer unless full and 
adequate consideration is received for making the expen-
ture" (Section 82015) must be reported. 
Free food and 
beverages should be reported on the candidate's campaign 
statement as a non-monetary contribution to his/her cam-
paign. 
If the value of the non-monetary contribution is 
$500 or more, the donor is an "expenditure committee" 
(Section 820l3(b» 
and must file campaign statements on 
the same schedule as the candidate. 
For your information I have enclosed a copy of the 
Political Reform Act with the referred to sections marked. 
Please let me know if I may be of additional assistance. 
RSK: 
TECHNICAL ASSIST ANCF Af)MINISTRA TlnN 
Very truly yours, 
Siciliano-Kutch 
Technical Assistance Program 
Coordinator

sections.conclusion: Free food and beverages should be reported on the candidate's campaign statement as a non-monetary contribution to his/her campaign. If the value of the non-monetary contribution is $500 or more, the donor is an "expenditure committee" (Section 82013(b)) and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.
sections.question_synthetic: How should free food and beverages contributed to a candidate be reported on campaign statements under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free food and beverages must be reported as non-monetary contributions on campaign statements. Donors of non-monetary contributions valued at $500 or more qualify as expenditure committees and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers but contains clear question and answer content embedded in the body text. The document includes a follow-up handwritten note from the requester. Confidence reduced due to document formatting issues and partial text OCR quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should free food and beverages contributed to a candidate be reported on campaign statements under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: Free food and beverages should be reported on the candidate's campaign statement as a non-monetary contribution to his/her campaign. If the value of the non-monetary contribution is $500 or more, the donor is an "expenditure committee" (Section 82013(b)) and must file campaign statements on the same schedule as the candidate.

The law makes no distinction between state and local all "expenditures made at the behest of a f ttee or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration is received for making the expen- ture" (Section 82015) must be reported. embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Raymond Pyke's inquiry regarding the reporting of free food and beverages contributed to a candidate, clarifying that such items constitute non-monetary contributions that must be reported on campaign statements.
